data.title: JW Marriott Singapore appoints Karl Hudson as general manager
data.description:
He recently served as the group’s Regional Vice President for Japan and Guam.

JW Marriott Singapore South Beach has appointed Karl Hudson as its new General Manager, the hotel announced in a press release.

Hudson, a hospitality veteran who has overseen the opening of more than 80 Marriott International hotels, most recently served as the group’s Regional Vice President for Japan and Guam.

He brings strategic vision and operational expertise to the 634-room property, which is part of Marriott’s luxury portfolio in Singapore’s Marina Bay.

He said his leadership will emphasise people, personalisation, and place-making, whilst preserving the hotel’s design-led identity.

data.title: Singapore amongst most stable APAC market for CFOs
data.description:
Only two CFO departures were recorded in H1.

Singapore remained one of the more stable markets in the Asia-Pacific (APAC) region for chief financial officer (CFO) appointments in the first half of 2025, according to Russell Reynolds Associates. This comes even as global CFO hiring hit a seven-year high.

The report recorded two CFO departures in Singapore during this period. APAC turnover overall stood at 6.2%, below the global rate of 9%.

Within the region, 57% of new CFOs were promoted internally, underscoring the strength of corporate succession planning. Singapore, tracked through companies on the Straits Times Index (STI), reflects this broader trend of relative stability.

However, gender diversity remains a concern. The index found that only one woman has been appointed CFO in Singapore in the past eight quarters, highlighting a persistent leadership gap.

By contrast, women accounted for 22% of CFO appointments across APAC and globally in the first half of 2025.

The study also observed that outgoing APAC CFOs served an average of 4.7 years, shorter than the global average of 6.2 years.

Meanwhile, 70% of new APAC CFOs were first-time appointees, a higher proportion than in other regions, which leaned toward hiring seasoned external candidates.

data.title: MAS sets standards for responsible financial advertising online
data.description:
It will be effective on 25 March 2026.

The Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) has issued new rules to guide financial institutions, advertisers, and content creators in sharing financial content responsibly online.

Effective 25 March 2026, the framework sets safeguards for digital media, requiring transparent disclosures and stronger policies to monitor online financial activities.

In collaboration with the Advertising Standards Authority of Singapore (ASAS), MAS also introduced the “7 must-knows when sharing financial information online.”

The guide explains when a licence may be required, what checks to conduct before promoting products, and how to disclose compensation.

Separately, MAS will issue advisory letters to five content creators who may have provided financial advice without approval.

These individuals have been instructed to revise their content in line with regulatory standards.

data.title: CapitaLand India Trust divests Chennai and Hyderabad assets in $162m deal
data.description:
The divestment is part of its capital recycling strategy to optimise portfolio quality.

CapitaLand India Trust (CLINT) has agreed to divest two Indian properties, CyberVale in Chennai and CyberPearl in Hyderabad, for approximately $161.7m (INR 11,031m).

The deal, CLINT’s first divestment since its 2007 listing, was struck at about a 3% premium to the assets’ independent valuations as at 31 December 2024. Net proceeds are expected to be $158.8m (INR 10,828m).

CLINT said the divestment is part of its capital recycling strategy to optimise portfolio quality and enhance financial agility.

CLINT CEO Gauri Shankar Nagabhushanam noted that the proceeds could be used to strengthen the balance sheet through debt repayment, redeployed into higher-yielding projects, or support distributions to unitholders.

Following the sale of the two properties, which have a combined floor area of 1.4 million square feet, CLINT’s completed portfolio will stand at about 21.2 million square feet.

Its holdings will include International Tech Park Chennai, three industrial facilities, and a data centre under development in Chennai, as well as International Tech Park Hyderabad, aVance Hyderabad, and a data centre under development in Hyderabad.

As of 30 June, CLINT managed $3.7b in assets, including IT business parks, industrial and logistics facilities, and data centre developments across several Indian cities.

data.title: Crédit Agricole CIB and Indosuez inaugurate new Singapore office
data.description:
They will share the 43,420 square feet workspace at IOI Central Boulevard Towers.

Crédit Agricole Corporate and Investment Bank and Indosuez Wealth Management today inaugurated their new Singapore office at the IOI Central Boulevard Towers.

The new shared office consolidates their operations in a 43,420 square feet workspace across two floors in the A-Grade office building.

Crédit Agricole CIB and Indosuez are both entities under Crédit Agricole Group.

The new Singapore office was selected to foster greater collaboration, flexibility and sustainability, in line with the group’s workplace enhancement initiative, said Antoine Sirgi, senior country officer for Singapore, Crédit Agricole Group.

The launch was timed to coincide with Crédit Agricole’s 120 years in Singapore and Singapore’s 60th year of independence, according to a press release.

data.title: OCBC appoints Carina Lee as group chief risk officer
data.description:
She will replace Noel Gerald DCruz.

OCBC has named Carina Lee as group chief risk officer (CRO) and member of the management committee, effective 1 January 2026.

She will replace Noel Gerald DCruz, who will retire on 31 December 2025 after 36 years with the bank.

Lee, currently group chief credit officer for wholesale banking, has 28 years of banking experience in business and risk management.

Since joining OCBC in 2021, she has overseen credit risks for the wholesale banking loan portfolio, spanning SMEs, large corporates, financial institutions, sovereigns, and counterparties.

She also served on the board of OCBC China from 2021 to 2025.

Before joining OCBC, Ms Lee spent more than 20 years with a global bank, holding senior roles in operational risk management, credit policy, and wholesale banking, including as deputy head of local corporates and head of the China Desk in Singapore.

data.title: Prime US REIT to raise $32m via private placement
data.description:
New units to be issued at $0.25–$0.26 per unit.

Prime US REIT will raise at least $32.2m (US$25m) through a private placement of new units priced between $0.25 (US$0.1935) and $0.26 (US$0.2000) each.

The placement is fully underwritten by DBS Bank and Maybank Securities and will be offered to institutional and accredited investors. Final pricing will be set after a book-building process.

The price range represents a discount of 7.0% to 10.0% to the last traded VWAP of $0.28 ($0.2150). About $31.2m ($24.2m), or 96.8% of the proceeds, will fund capital expenditure, tenant incentives and leasing costs to activate new leases and retain tenants. Around $1.0m (US$0.8m), or 3.2%, will cover fees and expenses.

The move supports stronger leasing activity, with over 400,000 sq ft of leases signed in 1H2025 and occupancy rising to 80.2%.

The funds will help activate leases and back another 150,000 sq ft in advanced negotiations, aiming to lift the REIT’s distribution payout ratio to at least 50% in 2H2025.

The placement will also lower aggregate leverage from 46.7% to about 45.9%, increase trading liquidity, and enlarge the unitholder base with roughly 127 million new units expected to be issued.

An advanced distribution of 50% of distributable income from 1 July to 5 October 2025 will be paid to existing unitholders before the new units are issued.

Listing of the new units on the SGX is targeted for 6 October 2025.

data.title: Seatrium unit, Cochin Shipyard ink deal to expand offshore services in Asia
data.description:
Seatrium brings engineering and offshore solutions.

Seatrium Offshore Technology Pte Ltd (SOT), a subsidiary of Seatrium Limited, has signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) with Cochin Shipyard Limited (CSL) to strengthen offshore cooperation in India and Asia.

The partnership combines SOT’s engineering expertise and offshore solutions with CSL’s shipbuilding, fabrication and repair capabilities to target Maintenance, Repair and Overhaul (MRO) projects and explore new offshore markets in the region.

“India’s rapidly developing offshore energy sector and rising demand for maritime infrastructure present compelling opportunities for collaboration and innovation,” said Winston Cheng, senior vice-president of SOT.

Sivakumar A, general manager of Ship Repair at CSL, said the collaboration will enable both companies to provide “high-quality, cost-effective offshore services” across the region.

The deal includes joint marketing, project execution and technology sharing, and follows a November 2024 agreement between Seatrium and CSL to design and supply equipment for jack-up rigs in India.

According to the International Energy Agency (IEA), India is projected to lead global oil demand growth, reaching an estimated 6.6 million barrels per day by 2030.

data.title: HSBC opens Quantum Centre of Excellence in Singapore
data.description:
It will focus on post-quantum cryptography and quantum key distribution.

HSBC has launched a Quantum Centre of Excellence (CoE) in Singapore, its second global hub after the UK, to drive quantum innovation and strengthen cybersecurity in financial services.

The Singapore CoE will focus on the practical applications of quantum technologies across banking and finance.

Early areas of research include post-quantum cryptography (PQC), quantum key distribution (QKD), and long-term infrastructure resilience, as well as exploring the potential of quantum computing in financial modeling, optimisation, and machine learning.

The centre will also act as a collaboration platform for regulators, researchers, and fintech partners to accelerate the development of quantum-safe solutions.

HSBC is already active in this space, participating in the industry-wide QKD sandbox for secure financial communications alongside the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS), DBS, OCBC, UOB, SPTel, and SpeQtral.

data.title: Singtel’s Optus hit with $84.9m fine over sales practices
data.description:
The penalty was fully accounted for in Optus' 2024/25 results.

The Australian Federal Court ordered Singtel’s Optus to pay a $84.9m (AUD100m) penalty for engaging in unconscionable conduct and inappropriate sales practices.

On 18 June, Optus Mobile Pty had settled with the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission, which included an Enforceable Undertaking and a proposed penalty.

Optus said the penalty was fully accounted for in its results for the year ended 31 March 2025.

($1=AUD1.17)
